navigation.title: Getting started
navigation.description: Understand Seonic, connect it to a Next.js app, and run the first inspection flow.

metadata.title: Getting Started
metadata.description: Learn what Seonic is, how it connects to a Next.js app, and what the first local workflow looks like.

hero.title: Start with the actual Seonic workflow
hero.lead: Seonic is a local SEO inspector for Next.js projects. Start here if you need a clear product summary, the current setup path, and the first commands worth running.

sections.title: What Seonic is
sections.lead: Seonic is not a hosted crawler or a release-only checklist. It is a local developer tool that helps teams inspect what each Next.js route is exposing before they ship.

blocks.content: The project is built around one simple idea: SEO data is easier to fix when engineers can see route output, warnings, and where the metadata came from in the same workspace.

items.title: Inspect existing metadata
items.body: Seonic scans app/ or src/app/, detects metadata exports and generateMetadata, and shows which routes already expose SEO values.

items.title: Add Seonic config where needed
items.body: Global defaults live in seonic.config.ts. Route-level metadata can be added with generated or hand-written .seonic.ts files next to route files.

items.title: Review before release
items.body: The current toolchain includes route warnings, score calculation, SERP and OG previews, sitemap generation, feed generation, and a dashboard for inspecting coverage.

sections.title: Quick start
sections.lead: The shortest path today is to run Seonic from the workspace and point it at a Next.js app with a config file.

items.title: Clone the Seonic workspace
items.body: The CLI and dashboard currently live inside the main workspace repository.
items.code:
git clone https://github.com/seoniclabs/seonic.git
cd seonic
pnpm install

items.title: Create a config file in your Next.js app
items.body: Point Seonic at the project you want to inspect. Set your base URL, SEO defaults, and the local Next.js dev server if you want live metadata fetched from rendered pages.

items.title: Start your Next.js app and Seonic
items.body: Run your app server first, then start Seonic with the config path for that project.
items.code:
# terminal 1 - inside your Next.js app
pnpm dev

# terminal 2 - inside the Seonic workspace
pnpm --filter seonic dev -- --config /absolute/path/to/your-app/seonic.config.ts

items.title: Open the dashboard and inspect routes
items.body: Visit http://localhost:6200 to review route inventory, warning counts, SEO scores, route previews, and generated sitemap or feed output.

sections.title: Core commands
sections.lead: The current CLI is narrow on purpose. It focuses on the local inspection loop and a small set of outputs teams actually need.

items.title: seonic dev
items.body: Starts the local dashboard, scans routes, validates metadata, and optionally fetches live metadata from the Next.js dev server.

items.title: seonic build
items.body: Scans the same route set and generates sitemap.xml and feed.xml using your Seonic config.

items.title: seonic generate
items.body: Creates .seonic.ts files for specific routes or for every eligible route, so teams can turn detected metadata into managed config.

sections.title: What the first session should answer
sections.lead: A useful first run is less about polishing copy and more about understanding the current SEO shape of the app.

items: Which routes are already configured and which routes are still unconfigured.
Whether metadata is coming from metadata, generateMetadata, or Seonic config.
Which warnings show up first: missing title, description, canonical, OG image, JSON-LD, hreflang, or duplicate metadata.
Whether the previewed output and generated artifacts match the route structure you expected.

sections.title: Current scope
sections.lead: The product direction is ambitious, but the current implementation is intentionally focused.

items: Detects existing metadata exports and generateMetadata with a read-only scan.
Supports global config, route-level .seonic.ts, dashboard previews, sitemap output, feed output, and file generation.
More precise source trace and broader framework support are still in progress.

blocks.title: Early product note
blocks.content: Seonic is still early, so the current workflow assumes a local workspace, a Next.js app, and a small CLI surface rather than a polished hosted onboarding flow.

cta.title: Need the product model next?
cta.lead: The overview page explains how route scanning, validation, previews, and generation fit together in the current Seonic design.
